---
title: Treatment Evidence for ME/CFS and Long COVID –- Clinical Summary
subtitle: Living Systematic Review — March 2026
author: Athanasia Monika Mowinckel
format: typst
---



### Evidence Base

- **36,577 studies screened** (automated + human verification pipeline)
- **340 treatment studies** with extracted quantitative data
  - 167 biomedical (316 effect sizes)
  - 173 psychological/behavioural (416 effect sizes)
- **177 GRADE assessments** completed
- **Quality ratings**: Cochrane ROB2 (RCTs), ROBINS-I (observational)

---

### Biomedical Interventions

#### Interventions with Most Evidence (by number of reported effect sizes)

| Intervention | Effect sizes | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Low-Dose Naltrexone | 28 | Primarily observational; targets neuroinflammation |
| Nirmatrelvir/Ritonavir | 21 | Mix of RCTs and observational; early treatment vs established LC |
| CoQ10/NADH | 14 | Mitochondrial energy support |
| COVID-19 Vaccination | 11 | Post-infection vaccination effect on LC symptoms |
| Apheresis | 10 | Blood filtering; small pilot studies |
| Creatine | 8 | Cellular energy supplementation |
| Nicotinamide Riboside | 7 | NAD+ precursor |
| Hypoxia Therapy | 7 | Intermittent hypoxic-hyperoxic training |
| HRG80 Red Ginseng | 7 | Adaptogen |
| Brain Stimulation | 5 | TMS, tDCS |
| Fluvoxamine | 5 | Sigma-1 receptor agonist; anti-inflammatory properties |
| SSRIs | 5 | Various agents |

**Additional agents investigated** (1-4 effect sizes each): metformin, methylphenidate, rapamycin, minocycline, probiotics, rintatolimod, rituximab, IVIg, hydrocortisone, stellate ganglion block, ketamine, colchicine, pirfenidone, SGLT2 inhibitors, among ~60 others.

#### Biomedical Outcomes by Domain

| Domain | Effect sizes | GRADE certainty |
|---|---|---|
| Fatigue | 75 | Very low |
| Physical function | 45 | Very low |
| Symptoms (general) | 30 | Very low |
| Prevalence/risk reduction | 25 | Very low |
| Cognition | 19 | Very low |
| Quality of life | 19 | Very low |
| Pain | 13 | Very low |
| Respiratory | 13 | Very low |
| Sleep | 13 | Very low |
| Mortality | 12 | Very low |
| Depression | 11 | Very low |
| Biomarker | 10 | — |
| Anxiety | 6 | — |
| Cardiovascular | 6 | — |
| PEM | 5 | Very low |
| Autonomic | 2 | Very low |

**All biomedical treatment evidence is GRADE "very low" certainty**, reflecting predominantly small sample sizes, heterogeneous interventions, high risk of bias, and imprecision.

Notable exception: **Metformin** has moderate-certainty evidence from the RECOVER-COVID trial for LC risk reduction.

---

### Psychological/Behavioural Interventions

#### Interventions with Most Evidence

| Intervention | Effect sizes |
|---|---|
| CBT | 50 |
| Exercise (general) | 46 |
| Pulmonary Rehabilitation | 37 |
| Rehabilitation (multidisciplinary) | 31 |
| Graded Exercise Therapy | 26 |
| Qigong/Tai Chi | 22 |
| Mindfulness | 18 |
| Yoga | 13 |
| Digital cognitive training | 8 |
| Self-management support | 7 |
| Pacing | 7 |

#### Psychological/Behavioural Outcomes by Domain

| Domain | Effect sizes | GRADE certainty |
|---|---|---|
| Physical function | 95 | Very low |
| Fatigue | 78 | Low |
| Quality of life | 41 | Low |
| Anxiety | 37 | **Moderate** |
| Depression | 34 | **Moderate** |
| Cognition | 23 | Very low |
| Self-efficacy | 18 | — |
| Symptoms (general) | 15 | Very low |
| Respiratory | 13 | Very low |
| Sleep | 13 | Very low |
| Work/social | 12 | — |
| Pain | 5 | Very low |
| PEM | 5 | Very low |

**Key finding**: CBT evidence achieves **moderate certainty for depression and anxiety** outcomes only. All physical outcome measures remain low or very low certainty.

---

### Paradigm Comparison

| | Psychological/Behavioural | Biomedical |
|---|---|---|
| Studies | 173 | 167 |
| Effect sizes | 416 | 316 |
| Highest GRADE | Moderate (depression, anxiety) | Moderate (metformin, select SRs) |
| Dominant designs | RCTs (larger, established tradition) | Mixed; many pilot/observational |
| Outcome measures | Self-report questionnaires | Mix: labs, clinical, self-report |
| PEM measured | 5/416 (1.2%) | 5/316 (1.6%) |

**Critical methodological divergence**: The two paradigms measure different constructs using different instruments in potentially different populations. Apparent differences in effectiveness may partly reflect measurement culture rather than true treatment superiority.

**Outcome measure heterogeneity**: Fatigue alone is measured by Chalder Fatigue Scale, MFI-20, VAS, FSS, and numerous others across studies, complicating pooling.

---

### PEM as a Stratifying Variable

Post-exertional malaise is the cardinal feature of ME/CFS (required by all modern diagnostic criteria) and reported in a substantial proportion of Long COVID patients. Our review identifies PEM as a critical moderator:

- **PEM-positive cohorts show lower recovery rates** than PEM-negative cohorts
- **Activity-increasing interventions (GET, exercise)** carry potential for harm in PEM-positive patients that goes undetected when PEM is not measured
- **Only ~1.4% of all treatment effect sizes** come from studies that assessed PEM
- Trials not assessing PEM cannot distinguish therapeutic benefit from patient deterioration masked by subjective questionnaire response

**Clinical implication**: PEM status should be assessed before prescribing activity-based interventions. The absence of PEM measurement in a trial does not mean absence of harm.

---

### GRADE Evidence Summary — Treatment Domains

| Treatment category | Outcome | Certainty | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| CBT | Depression | Moderate | Likely reduces depression symptoms |
| CBT | Anxiety | Moderate | Likely reduces anxiety symptoms |
| CBT | Fatigue | Low | May reduce self-reported fatigue |
| CBT | Physical function | Very low | Very uncertain effect |
| CBT | Quality of life | Low | May improve QoL |
| CBT | Cognition | Very low | Very uncertain effect |
| CBT | PEM | Very low | Very uncertain; minimal data |
| Biomedical (all pooled) | Fatigue | Very low | Very uncertain effect |
| Biomedical (all pooled) | Physical function | Very low | Very uncertain effect |
| Biomedical (all pooled) | Quality of life | Very low | Very uncertain effect |
| Biomedical (all pooled) | Cognition | Very low | Very uncertain effect |
| Metformin | LC risk reduction | Moderate | Likely reduces Long COVID incidence |
| Pulmonary rehab | Physical capacity (6MWD) | Moderate | Likely improves walk distance |
| Pulmonary rehab | Dyspnoea | Low | May reduce dyspnoea |
| LDN | Pain (fibromyalgia) | Low | May reduce pain |
| LDN | Fatigue (Long COVID) | Very low | Very uncertain effect |
| Nirmatrelvir/ritonavir | Post-acute mortality | Low | May reduce mortality |

---

### Evidence Gaps

1. **PEM measurement** is almost universally absent from treatment trials (<2%)
2. **Adverse event reporting** is inconsistent; absence of reporting ≠ absence of events
3. **Pacing** — the most commonly used patient strategy — has only 7 effect sizes (biomedical) + 7 (behavioural)
4. **Long-term follow-up** is rare; most trials report short-term outcomes
5. **Diagnostic heterogeneity** — studies use different ME/CFS and Long COVID case definitions
6. **Recovery definition variability** makes between-study comparison of prognosis unreliable
7. **No head-to-head trials** comparing biomedical and psychological approaches directly

### Clinical Implications

1. **No single treatment has high-certainty evidence** for any outcome in ME/CFS or Long COVID
2. **CBT has the strongest evidence base** but only for psychological outcomes (depression, anxiety); claims of physical function improvement rest on very low certainty evidence
3. **Activity-based interventions require PEM screening** — exercise prescription without PEM assessment risks harm
4. **Biomedical treatments are promising but premature** — LDN, antivirals, and metabolic agents need larger RCTs
5. **Multidisciplinary management with pacing as foundation** remains the most defensible clinical approach pending stronger evidence
6. **Publication bias assessment** (funnel plots, Egger's test, trim-and-fill) is conducted where k ≥ 5; results suggest potential positive-result publication bias in both paradigms

---

*Living systematic review. Random-effects meta-analysis (REML). Effect sizes: Hedges' g (continuous outcomes). Heterogeneity: I², τ², Q-statistic. Sensitivity: leave-one-out influence diagnostics. Data current as of March 2026.*
